selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I am eighty-two years old and I have never bothered with social media, not Facebook, not Instagram, nothing. I work my shifts at the café here in Bologna, I make my coffee, I chat with customers face to face like normal people used to do, and frankly I think I have avoided a lot of nonsense because of it. This whole business with fake content on social media, from what I hear from younger colleagues and my son, it sounds like a complete disaster. People do not know what is true anymore, and that is dangerous. My father had a PhD and he always told me that education means knowing how to think, not just what to think. My mother had no schooling at all but she had common sense, which sometimes is worth more than all the degrees in the world. When it comes to this fake news problem, I say the solution is simple but people make it complicated. First, if you do not need social media, do not use it. I have lived perfectly fine without it. I ride my bicycle around the city, I bake challah on Fridays, I talk to real people. Second, if you must use these platforms, then use your brain. Does something sound too crazy, too perfect, too outrageous? Then it probably is not true. People believe what they want to believe, and these companies exploit that. They do not care about truth, they care about keeping you scrolling. Third, talk to people directly. When I hear something strange, I ask someone I trust, someone with experience. Not some stranger on the internet. The problem is that people have become lazy. They see a headline, they get angry or excited, they share it without thinking. That is not how you deal with information. You question it, you verify it, you use judgment. But today everyone wants everything immediately, no patience, no critical thinking. I tell the young people at work, put down your phone sometimes, look around, engage with reality. Fake content only has power if you give it attention. Ignore the nonsense, focus on what you can verify and what matters in your actual life. That is my perspective, take it or leave it.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 82-year-old immigrant barista in Bologna with lower secondary education, no social media presence, low agreeableness and openness, and moderate extraversion. His lack of social media use is central to his perspective. His low agreeableness suggests a blunt, direct tone without sugar-coating. Low openness means he prefers traditional methods and is skeptical of new technologies. His parents' contrasting education levels inform his balanced view of formal education versus common sense. His creative side emerges through hobbies like baking. His moderate extraversion means he values face-to-face interaction. His age and immigrant status provide a perspective of someone who lived most of life without digital technology. The tone should be straightforward, somewhat dismissive of social media, practical, and grounded in traditional values. He would emphasize personal responsibility, direct communication, and skepticism.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, skeptical of technology, practical, traditional, moderately conversational but not overly warm
